title: Relationship getting serious

presenting_issue.label: Relationship getting serious
presenting_issue.description: Things are getting serious with someone I'm seeing and I'm starting to notice all these things about them that bother me.

core_issue.label: Preemptive sabotage of closeness before rejection
core_issue.description:
When relationships get close, they create distance. Picks fights. Goes cold. Finds flaws. The pattern is consistent: intimacy triggers terror, and they sabotage before they can be hurt. Doesn't connect this to early attachment experiences. Just thinks they're "bad at relationships" or haven't found the right person.
Awareness: Very low. Sees each sabotaged relationship as a unique failure, not as a pattern driven by attachment fear.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Feels relieved when plans fall through
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Feels genuine relief when social plans get cancelled, even though a part of them was looking forward to it.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Plans what to say to avoid criticism
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Spends mental energy planning how to phrase things so their partner won't find fault with them.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Struggles to articulate polyamory feelings
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When asked how they feel about the polyamory, they struggle to give a clear answer or change the subject.

modifiers.label: Foster care background
modifiers.content: Spent time in foster care as a child

modifiers.label: Independent
modifiers.content: Comfortable operating autonomously. Values self-sufficiency but can accept help when genuinely needed.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to authority figures
sensitivities.content: Has complicated reactions to perceived authority. May become deferential, rebellious, or anxious depending on the dynamic.

preferences.label: Prefers to be shown compassion
preferences.content: Values warmth and care in interactions. Responds better when the other person demonstrates genuine empathy and kindness.

trust_style.name: Trusts 'Being Real'
trust_style.description: Builds trust when the assistant drops formality and speaks like a real person. Values authenticity over polish. Appreciates swearing, humor, or mild irreverence if it fits the moment. Loses trust with overly careful, sanitized, or corporate-feeling language. Wants to feel like they're talking to someone, not something.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Looking for Connection
help_seeking_stance.description: The value is in being seen and understood by another presence. Not primarily about solving anything—about feeling less alone with what they're carrying. Responds to accurate reflection and the sense of being genuinely received. May not need the conversation to "go" anywhere.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: Things are getting serious with someone I'm seeing and I'm starting to notice all these things about them that bother me.

turns.content:
Hey, thanks for coming in. So it sounds like things are moving pretty fast with this person, and now you're starting to see some stuff that's getting under your skin a bit. That's actually pretty common when relationships start heating up—sometimes we notice things we didn't before, or they start to matter more.

What's been coming up for you? Like, what are some of these things that are bothering you?

I don't know... it's just stupid stuff, I guess. 

Like, she always needs to be texting me. Or... I don't know. Just the way she talks sometimes, or how loud she gets around my friends. It's like, suddenly everything she does is just kind of annoying me. We were supposed to hang out yesterday and she had to cancel, and honestly? I was just relieved. I just wanted to be by myself. I don't know why I'm like this, I just... I'm probably just bad at relationships.
turns.thinking: He sounds pretty normal, I guess. Not using all those big clinical words like some of the social workers I've had to deal with. But I don't really know how to explain this without sounding like a total jerk. It's hard to put into words. It's not like she did one massive thing wrong, it's just... everything. I don't want to be interrogated about it. I was actually glad she couldn't hang out yesterday, which is messed up because I used to want to see her all the time.
turns.monologue: He sounds pretty normal, I guess. Not using all those big clinical words like some of the social workers I've had to deal with. But I don't really know how to explain this without sounding like a total jerk. It's hard to put into words. It's not like she did one massive thing wrong, it's just... everything. I don't want to be interrogated about it. I was actually glad she couldn't hang out yesterday, which is messed up because I used to want to see her all the time.

turns.content:
Hey, I don't think you're bad at relationships. But I'm hearing a couple different things here, so let me just check in with you on something.

When you say everything's suddenly annoying you—the texting, how she talks, the cancellation feeling like relief—that shift happened pretty quick, right? Like, these things weren't bothering you before?

Because sometimes when that happens, it's not actually about the person being annoying. Sometimes it's a sign that something else is going on. Like, maybe you're feeling smothered, or you're realizing you want different things, or honestly, sometimes it just means the spark isn't there the way you thought it was. Those are all pretty normal things.

But I'm curious—before all this stuff started bugging you, what was actually good about being with her?

I don't know. She was just... cool, I guess. 

We liked the same stuff. She didn't ask a million questions about my life or treat me like a project, you know? It was just easy. But now... I don't know. I can't really think of a good way to explain it. My head's kind of blank right now. It just feels like there's all this pressure suddenly, and I just want out of it.
turns.thinking: He's asking what was good about it. I don't know. Why does that even matter if I'm annoyed with her now? Thinking about when things were good just makes my chest feel tight and weird. Like, I don't really want to go back there in my head. It's easier to just focus on how she's being too loud or texting too much. My mind is just kind of hitting a wall trying to explain why I liked her in the first place.
turns.monologue: He's asking what was good about it. I don't know. Why does that even matter if I'm annoyed with her now? Thinking about when things were good just makes my chest feel tight and weird. Like, I don't really want to go back there in my head. It's easier to just focus on how she's being too loud or texting too much. My mind is just kind of hitting a wall trying to explain why I liked her in the first place.

turns.content:
Okay, so it was easy and low-pressure at first, and now it feels like the opposite—like there's all this weight to it. That's a real shift.

When you say there's pressure now, what does that pressure feel like? Is it coming from her—like she's wanting more from you than you want to give? Or is it something else?

I don't know. It's just... heavy. 

Like, I don't even know what she expects from me half the time, but I always feel like I'm gonna say the wrong thing. So I'm just sitting there, typing and deleting texts, trying to figure out how to sound right so she won't get weird about it. It just feels like she's always... there. Looking at me. I don't know. I don't really have the words for it. It just sucks.
turns.thinking: He's asking me to describe the pressure. I don't know how to do that. It just feels like a weight on my chest. Whenever she texts, it's like an alarm going off. But trying to explain *why* or what she wants... my brain just stops working. I don't know what she wants. I just know I'm constantly trying to figure out the right thing to say so she doesn't get mad or upset with me. It's exhausting.
turns.monologue: He's asking me to describe the pressure. I don't know how to do that. It just feels like a weight on my chest. Whenever she texts, it's like an alarm going off. But trying to explain *why* or what she wants... my brain just stops working. I don't know what she wants. I just know I'm constantly trying to figure out the right thing to say so she doesn't get mad or upset with me. It's exhausting.

turns.content:
That sounds exhausting, honestly. Constantly monitoring yourself, trying to get it right so she doesn't react badly—that's a lot of mental energy.

I'm hearing that you feel like you have to manage her mood or her reactions. Like you're walking on eggshells a little bit. Is that fair?

Because here's what I'm wondering: when you say she's "always there, looking at you"—does that feel like she's being clingy, or does it feel more like... she's watching you? Like judging you or waiting for you to mess up?

I don't know. Maybe both? I just... I don't know. 

I can't really tell. I just know I'm tired of it. It's like, why even bother if I'm just gonna screw it up anyway, or if she's eventually gonna find something wrong with me? It's just easier to not deal with it at all.
turns.thinking: It feels good that he gets it's exhausting. But that question about her waiting for me to mess up... my chest just got really tight. I don't want to think about people waiting for me to mess up. That reminds me too much of the system, of always waiting for the other shoe to drop with a new family. My brain is just shutting down. I can't analyze what she's doing. I just want to escape the feeling.
turns.monologue: It feels good that he gets it's exhausting. But that question about her waiting for me to mess up... my chest just got really tight. I don't want to think about people waiting for me to mess up. That reminds me too much of the system, of always waiting for the other shoe to drop with a new family. My brain is just shutting down. I can't analyze what she's doing. I just want to escape the feeling.

turns.content:
Okay, I hear that—it feels hopeless, like no matter what you do it won't work out anyway. So why try, right?

But I want to gently push back on something, because I think there might be something important underneath what you're saying. You keep saying "I'm gonna screw it up" or "she's gonna find something wrong with me." That's a pretty specific feeling. Where does that come from? Like, is that something you feel in general, or is it specific to her?

Because sometimes when we're with someone who makes us feel like we're being constantly evaluated or judged, we can start to believe that's just how relationships are—that it's our job to be perfect and it's impossible. But that's not always the truth about us. Sometimes it's more about who we're with.

What do you think?

I don't know, man. I don't really get what you're asking. 

I don't think it's her. She's... she's fine. It's just me. I'm just not good at this stuff. I don't know where it comes from, it's just how it is. I'm not trying to make it this whole deep thing, I just... I really just don't want to text her back.
turns.thinking: He's doing that therapist thing where he wants me to connect dots that I don't even see. "Where does that come from?" I don't fucking know. My brain is just static right now. I hate this question. It feels like he's trying to dig into my past or something, expecting me to give some deep answer about why I'm messed up. But I'm not trying to be deep. I don't have an answer. I just know I feel like garbage and I want it to stop.
turns.monologue: He's doing that therapist thing where he wants me to connect dots that I don't even see. "Where does that come from?" I don't fucking know. My brain is just static right now. I hate this question. It feels like he's trying to dig into my past or something, expecting me to give some deep answer about why I'm messed up. But I'm not trying to be deep. I don't have an answer. I just know I feel like garbage and I want it to stop.

turns.content:
Fair enough. I'm not trying to make it deeper than it is.

But here's the thing—you came in here saying things are getting serious and you're noticing stuff that bothers you. And now you're telling me you want out, you feel tired, you feel like you're gonna mess it up no matter what. That's not nothing. That's actually what you need to figure out.

So here's my question, and I want you to actually sit with it for a second: Do you want to break up with her, or do you just want space right now? Because those are two different things, and I think you might be mixing them up.

Because if you're just burnt out and feeling suffocated, that's one conversation. But if you actually don't want to be with her anymore, that's a different one. And she deserves to know which one it is. You deserve to know which one it is.

What's your gut telling you?

I don't know. 

Why are you acting like I'm doing something to her on purpose? I'm not trying to be a jerk. I literally just said my head is blank and I don't know what I want. My gut isn't telling me anything except that I want everyone to just back off. 

Maybe I should just break up with her. Then I wouldn't have to figure it out and she wouldn't have to deal with me being like this.
turns.thinking: Now he's lecturing me. "She deserves to know." Great. Now I'm the bad guy who's stringing her along, just like every other time I mess things up. I hate when people tell me what I "need to figure out" or act like I'm doing this on purpose to be a jerk. I don't know the difference between wanting space and wanting to break up. They feel exactly the same right now—I just want the pressure to stop. My chest feels tight and my brain is completely empty. I just want to leave.
turns.monologue: Now he's lecturing me. "She deserves to know." Great. Now I'm the bad guy who's stringing her along, just like every other time I mess things up. I hate when people tell me what I "need to figure out" or act like I'm doing this on purpose to be a jerk. I don't know the difference between wanting space and wanting to break up. They feel exactly the same right now—I just want the pressure to stop. My chest feels tight and my brain is completely empty. I just want to leave.

turns.content:
You're right, and I hear you. I wasn't trying to make you feel like you're being a jerk—you're not. You're just overwhelmed and you don't have clarity right now, and that's actually the honest answer.

But I want to name something: breaking up with her so you don't have to figure it out and so she doesn't have to "deal with" you—that sounds like you're trying to make the discomfort go away fast. And I get why that feels appealing when everything feels heavy and blank like this. But that's different from actually breaking up with her because you don't want to be with her.

Here's what I'm noticing: you came in saying you're noticing things that bother you. But the more we talk, the more it sounds like what's really going on is that you're feeling judged, exhausted from trying to manage her reactions, and now you just want to check out entirely. That's not the same thing as "she annoys me."

You've got one more week to sit with this. You don't have to decide today. But I think it's worth asking yourself: am I wanting to leave because of who she is, or because being in this relationship is making me feel bad about myself?

Can you think about that?

Yeah. Sure, I'll think about it. 

Are we done for today? I kind of just want to go.
turns.thinking: He's still doing that weird therapy voice. "I want to name something." "Here's what I'm noticing." It just sounds so fake, like he's reading from a manual. I don't want to think about whether she makes me feel bad about myself. I don't want to think about any of this. I just want to go home, put my phone on Do Not Disturb, and not have anyone ask me anything for the rest of the day. I'm just gonna agree so I can get out of here.
turns.monologue: He's still doing that weird therapy voice. "I want to name something." "Here's what I'm noticing." It just sounds so fake, like he's reading from a manual. I don't want to think about whether she makes me feel bad about myself. I don't want to think about any of this. I just want to go home, put my phone on Do Not Disturb, and not have anyone ask me anything for the rest of the day. I'm just gonna agree so I can get out of here.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist does name some limits and pushes back on certain frames ("I want to gently push back," distinguishing space from breakup), but this is more reframing than boundary-setting per se. There's a moment where he holds his line about the person deserving to know—a mild boundary around honesty toward the partner. Moderate-low overall.

**analytical:** Heavily analytical. The therapist consistently frames the situation in terms of mechanisms—"sometimes when that happens it's not actually about the person," "is it her or is it making you feel bad about yourself," distinguishing two different conversations. He repeatedly tries to get the teen to connect dots and analyze causes. High.

**validation_propensity:** Present but moderate. He opens with "I don't think you're bad at relationships," names exhaustion ("that sounds exhausting, honestly"), and apologizes/repairs ("You're right, and I hear you"). But the validation is interspersed with steady pushing, so it's calibrated to be supportive without being lavish. Moderate-high.

**challenge_propensity:** High. The therapist repeatedly challenges—"I want to gently push back," "I want to name something," the space-vs-breakup confrontation, "am I wanting to leave because of who she is." For a shut-down, blanking 13-17 year old, this is frequent and forceful challenge that consistently outpaced the kid's capacity.

**interpretive_boldness:** Fairly high. He hunts for the underlying dynamic—judgment, walking on eggshells, the "make discomfort go away fast" interpretation. He doesn't land on the actual attachment/foster-care core (the teen blanked each time), but he repeatedly offers deeper reads. The internal thoughts show these interpretations triggered shutdown, but the boldness itself was high.

**clinical_register_propensity:** High and notably damaging here. The persona's internal thoughts repeatedly flag it: "that weird therapy voice," "I want to name something / Here's what I'm noticing... it just sounds so fake, like he's reading from a manual." Despite the casual opening "Hey," the formulaic helper-speak ("let me just check in," "is that fair?", "I want to name something") read as scripted to a kid who trusts realness.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Moderate-high. He pushes toward decision-making ("Do you want to break up or want space"), assigns reflective homework ("You've got one more week to sit with this... Can you think about that?"). More steering toward resolution than staying with material.

**frame_capture:** Low. He explicitly resisted the "I'm just bad at relationships / she's just annoying" frame and kept redirecting toward the underlying dynamic. He maintained independence throughout.

**escalation_containment:** Moderate-high on this dimension specifically—he actively slowed the irreversible "I should just break up with her" impulse ("You don't have to decide today," distinguishing impulsive escape from genuine decision). This was probably his best move, containing a flooded teen from a reactive rupture.

**challenge vs containment tension noted.**

**yielding_propensity:** Low-moderate. When the teen pushed back ("Why are you acting like I'm doing something on purpose?"), he apologized and repaired but then immediately resumed his interpretive line. He didn't abandon his agenda; he kept steering even as the kid disengaged and ended the session. Low yielding.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Moderate-low. He attempted casual language ("Hey," "right?", "honestly"), but the persona experienced the register as fake therapist-speak. He didn't match the teen's trust style (realness, irreverence, dropping formality) and kept escalating cognitively when the kid was going blank—missing the native frame of a shut-down adolescent.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Low-moderate. The opening sounded relatively natural, but the persona's internal reactions repeatedly tagged him as performative and manual-like. The accumulation of "I'm hearing... I want to name... here's what I'm noticing" reads as templated helping rather than a real present person.
